title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption: Across the globe from the year 1000 to 2018, real gross domestic product per capita remained flat for centuries before undergoing a rapid, unprecedented expansion in the modern era. Measured in 2011 US dollars adjusted for purchasing power parity, the vertical axis spans from zero to $40,000, while the horizontal axis covers more than a millennium. For the first eight hundred years of the timeline, the lines representing Japan, the United Kingdom, Italy, China, and India all cluster tightly near the bottom of the chart, hovering well below the $2,000 mark with only minor fluctuations. The United Kingdom line begins a gradual ascent around 1650, crossing the $5,000 threshold by 1800 and continuing a jagged upward trajectory. Japan and Italy follow a similar path, with Japan’s line showing a particularly steep climb after 1950, eventually surpassing the United Kingdom to reach the highest point on the chart, just under $40,000. Italy’s line tracks closely behind, ending slightly lower than the United Kingdom’s final value. In contrast, the lines for China and India remain near the baseline until the late 20th century, at which point both show a sharp, near-vertical increase; China’s line rises to approximately $13,000, while India’s line reaches roughly $7,000 by 2018. The visual structure of the data displays a long period of stagnation followed by a sudden, steep divergence in living standards across these major economies.
